planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Petite Aubique is a compact-to-medium fig suited to container growing or small gardens. In zone 9 it produces a breba crop in June–July and a main crop in August–September. The compact size makes it excellent for patio containers.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Petite Aubique is a French compact fig variety producing small to medium, teardrop-shaped fruits with violet to brown skin and amber flesh — a mild, delicate, honey-sweet flavor suitable for fresh eating and preserves. The compact tree habit (smaller than most standard fig varieties) makes Petite Aubique one of the better options for container growing in large pots or half-barrels on patios and decks. The smaller plant size does not compromise productivity significantly; established container-grown Petite Aubique can produce 50–100 fruits annually. The small fruit size (1–1.5 inches) makes them a good all-in-one fresh-eating fig — no slicing required. In zone 9 the compact habit and full-sun requirement make it an excellent choice for warm south-facing walls or enclosed courtyards where reflected heat can accelerate ripening. The name 'Petite Aubique' is also found in the literature as 'Petite Grise,' indicating possible synonym use in some regions.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers Association — Compact Fig Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Container Fig Production
University of California Cooperative Extension — Fig Cultivar Trial

planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown trees in spring; in zone 9 plant February–April. Dauphine is a main-crop variety producing its heaviest harvest in August–September in zone 9. The vigorous, large tree requires generous spacing. It is known for exceptional productivity and large fruit size.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, moderately fertile soil; Dauphine is a French fig variety named for the Dauphiné region of southeastern France, producing large, round to flattened fruits with violet to brown-purple skin and pink to reddish-amber flesh with a rich, sweet, mild flavor. The large fruit size (2–3 inches) is among the biggest in the fig world and makes Dauphine figs particularly impressive for fresh eating and market presentation. The vigorous, productive tree performs well in zone 9 heat and provides abundant harvests of the large, handsome fruits from August through September. Dauphine figs are best eaten fresh at peak ripeness; the large size can lead to cracking if late-season rains arrive during ripening. The French Dauphiné region has a tradition of fig cultivation in its milder southern valleys, and Dauphine represents the large-fruited table fig tradition of this region. In zone 9 the Gulf Coast conditions closely approximate the warm, humid conditions of the Dauphiné valleys.
sources: California Rare Fruit Growers Association — French Fig Cultivar Descriptions
University of California Cooperative Extension — Large-Fruited Fig Varieties
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Figs for the Gulf South

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant October–February for best establishment. Biloxi requires only 150–200 chill hours, making it the best choice for the warmest parts of zone 9b and zone 10. Plant two or more southern highbush varieties for cross-pollination and maximum production. Acidify soil to pH 4.5–5.5 with sulfur if needed.

directions: Plant in full sun in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), organically rich, well-drained, sandy soil; Biloxi is a very low-chill-hour southern highbush blueberry developed by USDA and Mississippi State University, requiring only 150–200 chill hours — among the lowest chilling requirements of any productive blueberry and making it suitable for the warmest areas of zone 9 and parts of zone 10. The medium-sized, light blue, firm berries ripen in April–May in zone 9 — among the earliest-ripening blueberries of the season — with a mild, sweet, and slightly tart flavor. The early ripening window allows Biloxi to produce before summer heat stresses the plant and before peak blueberry pressure from insects and birds. The vigorous, upright plant habit is well-adapted to Gulf Coast conditions. Biloxi was tested and released as a variety specifically suited to the Gulf South and Florida, giving it a track record of success in the climate conditions that characterize zone 9a and 9b.
sources: USDA ARS Small Fruits Research Station — Biloxi Blueberry Release Notes
Mississippi State University Extension — Southern Highbush Blueberry Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blueberry Varieties for North Florida

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant October–February. Flordablue requires only 150–200 chill hours and is one of the earliest-ripening blueberries in the southeast. It benefits from cross-pollination with another low-chill variety like Sharpblue or Biloxi. Soil pH of 4.5–5.5 is essential — amend with elemental sulfur well in advance of planting if pH is too high.

directions: Plant in full sun in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), organically rich, well-drained, sandy soil; Flordablue is a very low-chill-hour southern highbush blueberry developed by the University of Florida, requiring only 150–200 chill hours — one of the standard blueberry varieties for zone 9 and zone 10 gardens where inadequate winter chilling prevents northern varieties from producing. The medium-sized, firm, dark blue berries ripen in March–May in zone 9, among the very earliest blueberries of the season. Flordablue produces reliably in the mild winters of north-central and north Florida and the Gulf Coast, where more northerly varieties like Bluecrop or Blueray would fail to receive adequate winter chill. The flavor is mild, sweet, and pleasant — not as intensely flavorful as northern highbush varieties grown in their optimal cool climates, but very acceptable for fresh eating and processing. As a University of Florida release, it has an extensive track record of performance in Gulf Coast conditions.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Flordablue Blueberry
Florida Blueberry Growers Association — Low-Chill Variety Guide
University of Georgia Extension — Southern Highbush Blueberry Production

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall or early spring; in zone 9 plant October–February. Windsor requires 500–600 chill hours and is best suited for the colder areas of zone 9a where winter temperatures regularly provide adequate chilling. It is not recommended for zone 9b or warmer zones. Cross-pollination with another variety is beneficial for maximum yield.

directions: Plant in full sun in very acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), organically rich, well-drained, sandy soil; Windsor is a southern highbush blueberry requiring 500–600 chill hours — more than the ultra-low-chill varieties like Flordablue and Biloxi, but still significantly less than northern varieties that require 800–1,200 hours. This intermediate chill requirement makes Windsor suitable for zone 9a where winters typically provide 500–700 chill hours but unsuitable for the warmer zone 9b areas. The advantage of Windsor's higher chill requirement is higher fruit quality — the berries are larger, firmer, darker, and more flavorful than very-low-chill varieties, with the rich, complex blueberry flavor that intensifies with more winter dormancy. Windsor was developed to provide the best possible fruit quality while still meeting the reduced chill requirements of the upper Gulf Coast. The midseason ripening (May–June in zone 9) follows the earliest varieties and provides an extended blueberry harvest season when planted alongside Biloxi or Flordablue.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — Windsor Blueberry Variety Notes
North Carolina State University Extension — Southern Highbush Blueberry Varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blueberry Variety Selection for North Florida
